conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the eligibility rules to ensure I can guide you accurately.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a child, Alex, who's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few more questions.

Do you live with Alex in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK and Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great. A few more questions to complete the assessment:

Are you currently the only person claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or is someone else (such as another parent or guardian) already receiving Child Benefit for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Just one more question to complete my assessment:

Is Alex currently in hospital for more than 12 weeks, or in care for more than 8 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in care for a couple of months now, but they come home to stay with me on weekends and spend quite a bit of time at home each week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. This is an important detail. To assess this correctly, I need to understand more specifically:

How many nights per week does Alex typically spend at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex usually comes home and spends a couple of nights with me each week - they're home with me on the weekends.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Based on what you've told me, I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me summarize your situation:
- You have a child, Alex, who is 8 years old
- You live in the UK
- Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Alex has been in care for a couple of months (more than 8 weeks)
- Alex comes home and spends 2 nights with you each week (on weekends)

According to the rules, normally after 8 weeks in care, you would no longer be entitled to Child Benefit. However, there is an exception: you may be able to claim Child Benefit if the child spends 2 nights in a row each week at home.

Since Alex spends 2 nights with you each weekend (which are consecutive nights), you meet this exception criteria.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news!** You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Although Alex has been in care for more than 8 weeks (which would normally affect your entitlement), you qualify under an important exception: you can continue to claim Child Benefit because Alex spends 2 consecutive nights at home with you each week.

This exception exists specifically for situations like yours where a child in care regularly spends time at home with their parent or guardian.

If your circumstances change in the future (for example, if Alex's care arrangements change), you should contact HMRC to update them.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), and the claimant lives with Alex in the UK and is responsible for them. Although Alex has been in care for more than 8 weeks (which would normally end Child Benefit eligibility), there is an exception that applies: the claimant can continue to claim Child Benefit because Alex spends 2 nights in a row each week at home (on weekends). This meets the exception criteria specified in the rules for children in care who spend at least 2 consecutive nights per week at home.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Despite Alex being in care for more than 8 weeks, the claimant qualifies under the exception for children in care who spend at least 2 consecutive nights per week at home, which Alex does on weekends.
